speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the journey.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Now, the Atlantic tried to leave Tulcea, immediately broke down. What happened was the Atlantic broke down, and the other ships-- the two other ships-- that is to say the [? Milos ?] and the-- what was the name of the other ship? The [? Milos ?] and the Pacific left on their own. And there was some-- there was some engine or-- some engine problems. And, as a result, the Atlantic left a few days later or a day or something later on its own. And without any trouble, we reached Istanbul. And a day later, we went through the Black Sea. Istanbul-- at Istanbul, we were boarded by the Turkish police and we got bread. We always got bread and water from the Jewish community. They supplied us with bread and water. We had some staples like sausage and-- and other stuff, and canned food that was a staple. But the boat came along with bread and with water. So we got bread and water. Then we continued. And we continued through the Aegean Sea. And, as you know, there are all these Greek islands. We stopped first at, I think, Mytilene, which used to be called, I think, at one time, Lesbos, and at Chios and at Ios and various other small Greek islands. And whenever we stopped, we got, from the Greek Port Authorities, bread and water. They, of course, knew who we were. We were-- because this was not the first transporter that had gone through. They knew who we were, and they were kind enough to supply us for free, I think, with bread and water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Greeks-- I must say the Greeks, I have high regard for Greek-- for the Greeks since then. They supplied us with food for free. Also, I remember one-- one incident at one of those islands. We had arrived there, and we got the bread and the water, and then we continued on our way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we were sailing out, there was this Greek-- [CRYING] there was this Greek priest standing there, on a-- on the edge of the port. I knew it was a Greek priest because they have a very specific dress. And he stood there-- [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Anyway, he raised his hands and he kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he kind of blessed the ship. So, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] anyway, we continued through these Greek islands, and arrived at-- we arrived at Heraklion in Crete. We arrived at Heraklion in Crete on October the 16th-- on October 16th. And then we ran out of coal. Our ship was a coal ship, by the way, and did not have a radio. As I told you, the conditions on the ship were terrible, but I don't have to go through this again. But we ran out of coal, and could continue any further. We did not have enough coal. So we were standing there for several weeks. In the meantime, what happened was that the war caught up with us. What happened was, on-- on-- I have to check my dates again. On October-- we arrived in Heraklion on October the 16th. On October the 28th, Italy declared war on Greece. And there were-- every day, there were air raid alarms. Not air raid attacks, there were air raid alarms. But fortunately there were no air raid attacks. We were standing in the-- in the port. I mean, there was nothing-- So the Greek government, of course, did not want us there, because they-- Greek-- Greece is a small country. They had enough trouble to fight the war against the big power like Italy. So they wanted to get rid of us. So eventually-- eventually-- I don't know what went on behind the scenes-- somehow we got coal. One fine day, a barge arrived with coal, and it was loaded on to the ship and we sailed. We sailed east, in the easterly direction.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we sailed east, and-- of course the captain, in charge of the ship. We sailed for about a day or two, and then the captain said, we have to go back, because we don't have enough coal. We can't make it to Palestine, or not even to Cyprus or anywhere else. There is not enough coal there. So-- but we didn't want to go back, you know? Because we didn't want to go back to enemy country, because we were already on our way to Allied territory. But he said we had no coal. What happened was the ship came to a standstill. The ship stopped in the middle of the ocean. So what do you do? What we did was, our leadership-- most of these people in our leadership, or some of them, were Czech. And one of them was a Czech Air Force officer who know, somehow-- who had a good idea on how to operate a ship. So, in other words, we arrested the captain and he took over the ship, together with the first Greek-- with the First Officer, who was also a Greek, who decided to cooperate with us. And we continued. However, how do you continue when you have no coal? So what happened then, we decided to break up the ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ship was, to a certain extent, made out of wood. All the decks and the interior partitions, everything was made out of wood. So we started to take all the wood-- we ripped up all the decks and took out all of the partitions-- the cabins, all the-- everything made out of wood was ripped up and cut into little pieces. And there was a long chain of people going into the engine room. And all those pieces were sent down into the engine and [INAUDIBLE] thrown into the-- into the boiler-- into the furnace. And in this fashion, we were able to continue for a few more days. We-- the ship was going a little bit, and then it came to a standstill, because there was not enough steam there. Then we chopped up more wood, got it back in, and continued again. And this went on for a few more days until we eventually saw land in the distance. But just before we did that, during that period-- by the way, our ship did not have a radio. We were-- there were no ways we could-- could communicate with anybody, and there was no way we could get help from anybody. But during those few days when we were in this-- in this situation, we were caught by two destroyers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two British Destroyers, fortunately. They were not Italians but British. They came along, and they came with great speed, and they circled around and they [? semaphored ?] with this thing here and said, who are you? So we said back, we are Jewish refugees and we need help. So they said, I'm sorry, we can't help you. Because they couldn't. I mean, what can they do? But they said they will-- we will-- we will radio that-- that you are in this situation. And they-- and they said goodbye and they took off. [LAUGHS] They took off in a big rush.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we continued a little bit further until we-- until the wood gave totally out and we were in sight of land. In other words, in the far distance we could see some mountains, and we had no more wood. So we were stopped. And then we were waiting for somebody to come from there and pick us up and tow us in. And that's what happened. A few hours later, a motorboat came out and it was flying the British flag, so we knew that we had reached-- somebody said we are in Cyprus. That land over there is Cyprus. So we had reached Cyprus. So was-- so it was great excitement. We had reached, finally, the Allied territory. So by that, we had escaped from Nazi Germany. That was-- that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the condition of the people on the ship at this time? Was there a lot of illness? Was there-- What was the morale like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there wasn't. The conditions were terrible, but people were bearing up. Except, of course, some-- some got sick and died-- about 10 or 12. But they were buried oversea. They were thrown overboard. And-- and-- but, you know, there was no time to think about things like this. You had to-- you were busy all the time, running up for food or whatever. There was no time to worry about things. You don't worry about things. You just try to keep alive. And there's no heroism or anything like this. You do-- you take it as it comes, from-- from hour to hour. There's nothing-- you don't worry about what is going to happen tomorrow, because you don't know what's happen-- going to happen in half an hour. That's all I can tell you. There's no time to think about these things. Philosophizing, you-- there is no time. You-- you just carry on as best as you can. You line up for your food and then you see where you can wash yourself and maybe you can-- maybe you can visit my parents, and then you are on other duty, or you have to stand here for a [? few ?] hours and watch the people don't cross the bridge, because if they cross the bridge they can beat the queue, which is lined up from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there's always something to do. Or then you have to sleep for a few hours at night, or then it rains. Then you have to go under, or you go below. So there's always something to do. That's it. [LAUGHS] What can I tell you? There's no time. So you reached Cyprus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we reached Cyprus. So the British sent out a-- a-- a-- a tugboat, and they towed us in, because we couldn't do it on our own. We had no-- no engine, and no engine power. So they-- they towed us in to port. The port is Limassol, the big port in Cyprus, and we were--

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first thing the British was that they arrested the captain and the whole crew, because you know-- because they were considered smuggling illegal immigrants into British territories. So they were immediately arrested, and I don't know what happened to them afterwards. I think they were tried, and they get a few months in jail or whatever. I have no idea. Then the British said, well, who are you? Of course they knew who we were, because the other two ships had arrived before. Right? So they knew, but they said we are-- we are Jewish refugees from-- from Germany and other countries here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, well, all right, we'll send you on to Palestine, and you'll get some food. But first of all, for the food you have to pay. See, the Greeks had supplied us with food for free. The British, we had to pay. So, in other words, everybody-- I-- everybody had either one pound-- either-- we were allowed to take out of Germany either one pound or $5.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had, I think, one pound and my parents each had $5. That-- I think some of it had to be handed over to pay for the food-- bread and other food-- that-- that we got from the British. They were very stingy, I must tell you. That was-- that-- everybody was very angry about it. I think even some people had to hand over some of their jewelry. But that I don't-- that I'm not sure about. But I remember we had to hand our-- hand over what little money we had. And also we had a big box of German drugs that we were allowed to take. And these are-- [INAUDIBLE] we handed that also over to the British, which was very valuable, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the British supplied us with coal and with a British crew, with a British captain, and coal, and we sailed for Palestine, which is very close from Cyprus to-- to Haifa is just a-- just a day. And we sailed for Palestine. And we saw the-- the promised land [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the distance. We saw the port and Mount Carmel in the distance. Everybody was singing the Hatikvah on deck. It was a great moment. I can assure you that nobody who on that ship will-- [CRYING] Anyway, so we arrived in Haifa, and-- and the British police came on board and they said, well, the other two ships had arrived. But the problem is there is no-- no place on land.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you have to go to another ship, but you'll be very comfortable there. Now usually, what the British had done in the past, what they had done was they-- people were-- with illegal immigrants, that is to say-- they put them into a camp and then-- then they let them go, but they deducted the number from the monthly immigration quota. There was a certain number of people that were allowed to go to Palestine. If illegal came, they deducted the-- you know? But nobody was deported. You know? So we figured the same thing was going to happen to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But not this time, apparently. What they-- what they said-- the British said, look, there is no space in the camp. You have to go on this other ship. And it-- you will be transshipped from-- because you can't stay on this terrible ship. It's all over crowded and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So always 200 people are supposed to get their things together and you will be transshipped from your ship to this big ship which was-- which was standing on the other side of the port. The ship is called the Patria, a French passenger liner which was confiscated by the British and used as a-- as a transport. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the first 200 people packed their bags, and they went down on a big barge, and they went over to the-- to the Patria. They went over, then the barge came back empty, and picked up the second 200, and they left for the Patria.

speaker: interviewee
text: They left for the Patria, but we couldn't really see the Patria. We could see only the three funnels at the other side of the port. And then suddenly, those three funnels disappeared. They were just gone. We didn't hear any sound or explosion or anything. But what happened then, afterwards, the last group of people who had been transshipped, or were supposed to be, they came back. And they were all crying and said the ship has capsized, and there are all people swimming in the water, and terrible things are happening, and so forth. So we knew that something terrible had happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened next was that the British decided, well, we couldn't stay onboard the ship, they're going to-- to send us to a camp after all. So we were taken off the ship, and my father and I went to a camp-- like a detention camp or whatever. A camp with barracks in Acre.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother, for one reason or another, went to a ladies' camp-- a women camp in Atlit, which is just outside of-- of Haifa, if you know the place. And there we stayed for about two weeks. And there we recovered some.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we had comparatively good food and we were able to take showers and all of that. And we had breakfast and lunch and dinner, which I'm sure it was very plain, but it was very good. Probably British Army food, I would assume. [INAUDIBLE] We really were able to clean ourselves again after being on that ship for about two months or whatever, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived-- by the way, we arrived in-- in Haifa-- let me see-- we arrived in Haifa on November the 24th. So we had left Tulcea-- we had left Tulcea on October the 7th of 1940, and we arrived in Haifa on November the 24th. So that's the period we were on that-- on that-- on the terrible ship, on the Atlantic. It's a long time, you know, in those conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in this camp, and we were quite happy there because we figured, you know, OK, we'll stay in the camp. Maybe we'll stay another two months or whatever, and then we'll be discharged. You know? Great. But that was not to be. The British, for one reason or the other-- political or whatever-- they were fighting alone. You remember France was defeated? They probably didn't want to have any problems with the Arabs. There are 3,500 people come that are illegal, and they-- you know, the political troubles.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they said, well, all these people are going to be deported. As a matter of fact, all these people who had already been on the-- on the Patria, they also were supposed to be deported. But then, because there was this terrible accident, or this-- the Patria capsized, the British government modified their decision. They said as follows-- those people who had been on the Patria and who had been saved-- 250 died, by the way. 250 drowned. But there were about-- about 1,500 or 1,700 people on the Patria, plus the crew and police and so forth. Those people who had been-- those refugees who had been on the Patria, they could stay in Palestine. They were going to be pardoned, so to speak. But the 1,700 or so who were on the Atlantic-- who had come on the Atlantic and had not been on the Patria yet, they are still going to be deported. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with the Jewish authorities in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only very little. There were a few Jewish auxiliary police at the camp, and these were the only people we were in touch with. And these Jewish auxiliary police, they were the one who told us that you're going to be deported. And what they told us was that you're going to be deported, and what you should do, you should offer passive resistance. You shouldn't dress yourself. You should not pack. You should offer passive-- you can't offer active resistance, but at least you should offer passive resistance. So what the-- what the majority of people did, they didn't dress and they-- they stayed naked. They stayed naked. They didn't pack anything. And so the British made an announcement saying, next day, everybody has to be packed. Breakfast will be served at 5:00 in the morning, and you have to be packed for transportation. They didn't say anything, where or what. They just said you have to be packed. You'll be transported somewhere else. But we had already heard from the Jewish police that they were going to deport us. Not-- nobody knew where to, but they were going to deport us. And this was very much opposed by the-- by the Jewish people of Palestine, because it was supposed to be the Jewish homeland.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're supporting Jewish refugees from the Jewish homeland? You know what I mean? This-- this was unheard of. Never had been ever-- no one had ever been deported by the British before. OK, we came-- we came as illegals, but even so. You know? So they offered passive resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Passive resistance. So what they-- what-- so what they did was that the camp was encircled by a British military brigade. They set up machine guns all over the place, and then the police-- then British police arrived. The military didn't involve themselves. The British-- Pale-- the sort of Palestine police, they came about 30 or 40 people per barrack. There were about-- it was 30 or 40 people-- 30 people per barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the police came with overwhelming force. They decided to clean out barrack by barrack. So they came, they cleaned out on barrack. If-- if people were not dressed, they took them, wrapped them in a blanket, and threw them on the truck. Once they had 10 or 15 people on a truck, the truck took off. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that's the way they operated. In this way, they were able to-- to clear off-- it took about-- took about, I don't know, five, six hours, to-- to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the British?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was a very specific incident. You know? Then, [LAUGHS] believe it or not, we had to go through a customs examination. But this is really unheard of. You know, what happened was this so-called passive resistance collapsed after about a few hours. This was also done only by the younger people. When they came to our hut, which was near the end, the passive resistance had already collapsed, and my father and I, we were dressed. So when we're dressed, we're put on a truck, and going to the port.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the port, we had to go through a customs examination. I mean, this is really incredible. So-- so I had to-- I had [INAUDIBLE] my luggage. He says, you come to customs. Open your bag, please. [LAUGHS] I had to open that. He says, what's this? I had a gel for hair cream or what that. He says, that-- somebody could-- something could be in the hair cream. He took that away.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of stuff got stolen, you know, by the way. Some cameras and valuable things were stolen, especially of those people who left completely naked, because they left everything behind. It was all gathered up by some-- some Arab prisoners whom the British had assembled for this purpose. All the stuff was gathered up in just heaps and sent off to-- to the ship. And there were two ships there, the [? Johann David ?] and the New Zealand. They were two Dutch ships whom the Brit-- whom the British had confiscated. And those two ships sailed for Mauritius within a day or so. My father and I, we were on one ship. I was on the [? Johann David, ?] I think, my father and I, and my mother was on the other ship. Because-- my father and I we were in Acre, and my mother was in Atlit. They were different camps, so we got on two different ships. But the ships sailed-- the ships left at the same time and sailed for Mauritius. It took about two weeks to get there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a long trip, because you have to go through-- through the Swiss canal, then all through the Red Sea, right? Then through the Indian Ocean, and all zig-zag because of the U-boat. The ships went zigzagging. So it takes-- therefore it takes much longer unless you-- you don't go straight.

speaker: interviewee
text: It took about-- I don't know. We arrived at [INAUDIBLE]. We left the-- the-- the ships left-- left-- the ships left on December the 9th, and arrived in Mauritius on December the 26th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter the war on that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We did not encounter a U-boat or anything else. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions on that ship like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was pretty good. We had-- [LAUGHS] except we had-- we had la-- we had mutton every day. After-- after-- after three or four days I couldn't eat mutton anymore. We had mutton for twice a day. Not for breakfast, but for lunch and for dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Afterward, we went on strike. We couldn't eat mutton anymore. They must have had a tremendous amount of mutton on board, frozen, you know? I couldn't eat it anymore. [LAUGHS] Did you know at that point where they were taking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't remember. I think we found out that it's going to Mauritius, but I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the people on the boat? How-- what was the morale like? What were they feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Low. It was-- everybody was very depressed. You know, what are we going to do? What-- what-- Mauritius? Who has ever heard of a thing of this place? I mean, what do people know about Mauritius? I knew about Mauritius only one thing, there's a famous stamp. The blau-- the blau-- the blue Mauritius stamp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's a tiny island somewhere in the Indian Ocean. What are we going to do there? What's-- you know? Although we were saved from Germany. OK, that's fine. Great. But, I mean, what are we going to do on this tropical island far away from anywhere? And who knows how we-- how long are we going to stay there and all that? It was very-- it was a very depressing thing. What happened once you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once we-- we-- we got there and we, of course, immediately unloaded onto buses or whatever. And we were brought to our camp. Now, our camp was actually in a place called [? Bobasan ?]. Let me say, Mauritius is a beautiful, tropical island with vegetation like you've never seen. There is green and wild flowers to very steep mountains, and they have green right to the top. It looks like a tropical paradise. Absolutely. These wild flowers and flowering trees you've never seen. It's absolutely magnificent.

speaker: interviewer
text: It has become, now, I understand, a big holiday resort. People fly there from Paris. They have regular flights from Paris to Mauritius. People fly there, you know? They have their-- these camps. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time, it didn't exist. At that time, there was only-- you could only go there by boat. So we arrived at our camp. The camp was really not a camp. It was a prison. What the-- what the British had done, they had cleared the prison of the prisoners, and we were sent to this prison. Now, the prison was organized with two huge cell blocks. Each cell blocks had about 300 or 400 cells-- each individual cells, and a wall around. And there were several other buildings in there, like workshops and storage and so forth. But they also had built a-- another camp adjacent to it for-- with barracks for about 30-- 30 persons each. And the way it was organized so all the men were in the prison with the cells, while the women were in the adjacent camp. In other words, each man had a cell to himself, while the women were about 20 or 25 women to one barrack. And that's the way, actually, it stayed for-- for the whole time, for the rest of the period we were in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you under guard there, or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. We were never allowed to go. We were under guard by the local police. But the-- the-- the sergeant and the-- and the upper-- upper echelon, so to speak, were all British. So the-- the camp commandant [INAUDIBLE] the certain captain armitage was a British police captain. And there were several police sergeants that had accompanied us from Palestine and who stayed there for four years, actually. There were two police-- two or three police sergeants from the Palestine police-- British Palestine police-- who stayed with us in Mauritius.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated there? Were you treated like prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We were treated like internees. Let's say it this way. We had complete freedom inside the camp. You know? Nobody bothered us inside the camp. We were not allowed to go out. We had-- you know, we could send as many letters as we wished and-- and so forth. We had our own kitchens and our own cultural activities.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't go into this a little bit more in greater detail, of course. Right? It took some time to get-- get organized. So we arrived first, it was-- first of all, in the first instance everybody was very depressing because what was the future going to be? We are in this camp on this-- on this godforsaken island.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are no Jews, by the way. Not one Jew living in Mauritius. Not one. Most of the population is black-- is-- is Creole. The-- the-- the language of the country is a kind of a Creole French, because at one time, Mauritius was French until Napoleon lost the war, and then it became a British colony.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, at that time-- at that-- and now it's an independent state, but at the time it was a British colony. And there were also many Indians living there. The main-- the main industry in Mauritius-- even today, I think-- is the sugar. Sugar in the cane-- sugar cane, which is being-- being cut down and-- and refined and then sent to England, most the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were-- we were cut off from the local population. We were living in this-- in this camp for-- for four years, and had very little contact with-- with the local population because we were not allowed to go out. Except once in a while, we got a day pass to go in to town for a day. But we had to go back-- we had to be back in in the evening. And once in a while we were allowed to go to a beach to go swimming, under escort-- under police escort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stop you there for a moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]
